misconceptionSummary: Students confuse multiples with factors, listing numbers that divide into the target rather than the target's times-table members.

lines.text: These are FACTORS of 12 — not multiples

steps.narration: The student listed the numbers that divide into twelve — those are factors. Multiples are the opposite: numbers that twelve divides into. Multiples of twelve are always twelve or larger.

visuals.ruleText: Factors of n are smaller than or equal to n — they divide INTO n
visuals.subText: Multiples of n are n or larger — n divides INTO them

steps.narration: Factors divide into the number — they are smaller. Multiples are what the number divides into — they are larger. Twelve is both a factor and a multiple of itself, but everything else is on one side or the other.
The correct multiples of twelve are twelve, twenty-four, thirty-six, forty-eight, sixty, and so on — the twelve times table. Each is divisible by twelve with no remainder.

visuals.label: Multiples ≥ n — n divides into them. Factors ≤ n — they divide into n.

steps.narration: Twelve, twenty-four, thirty-six — multiples grow upward from the number. Factors shrink downward toward one. The two lists go in opposite directions.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: listing factors (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12) when asked for multiples of 12. Multiples are 12, 24, 36, … — they go upward. Factors go downward toward 1.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Multiples and factors are opposites in direction. Factors of 12 are smaller: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12. Multiples of 12 are larger: 12, 24, 36, 48 and so on.
